name: Nature & Animals
description: Discover the wonders of wildlife, habitats, and the natural world!

questions.text: What is the largest land animal on Earth?
questions.explanation: The African elephant is the largest living land animal, with males weighing up to 14,000 lbs.

questions.text: What do you call an animal that eats only plants?
questions.explanation: A herbivore is an animal that feeds exclusively on plants and plant matter.

questions.text: How many legs does a spider have?
questions.explanation: Spiders are arachnids and have 8 legs, unlike insects which have 6.

questions.text: What is the fastest land animal?
questions.explanation: The cheetah is the fastest land animal, reaching speeds of up to 70–75 mph in short bursts.

questions.text: What is a group of lions called?
questions.explanation: A group of lions is called a pride, typically consisting of related females and their cubs.

questions.text: Which bird is known for its ability to mimic human speech?
questions.explanation: Parrots, especially African Grey Parrots, are well known for their ability to mimic human speech.

questions.text: What is the largest ocean in the world?
questions.explanation: The Pacific Ocean is the largest and deepest ocean, covering about one-third of Earth's surface.

questions.text: Which reptile is known for being able to change its skin color?
questions.explanation: Chameleons can change their skin color for communication, temperature regulation, and camouflage.

questions.text: What is the main diet of a giant panda?
questions.explanation: Giant pandas eat almost exclusively bamboo, consuming up to 84 pounds of it per day.

questions.text: What type of animal is a dolphin?
questions.explanation: Dolphins are marine mammals; they breathe air, give birth to live young, and nurse with milk.

questions.text: What is the term for animals that are active during the night?
questions.explanation: Nocturnal animals are active primarily during the night, like owls, bats, and raccoons.

questions.text: Which is the largest bird in the world by height?
questions.explanation: The ostrich is the tallest and heaviest bird, standing up to 9 feet tall.

questions.text: What is the term for the process by which plants make food using sunlight?
questions.explanation: Photosynthesis is the process by which plants use sunlight to convert CO₂ and water into food.

questions.text: How do bees communicate the location of flowers to other bees?
questions.explanation: Honeybees perform the waggle dance to communicate the direction and distance of food sources.

questions.text: What is the largest species of shark?
questions.explanation: The whale shark is the largest fish species, growing up to 40 feet long; it feeds on plankton.

questions.text: What do we call an animal that can live both on land and in water?
questions.explanation: Amphibians like frogs and salamanders can live both on land and in water.

questions.text: Which continent is home to the Amazon Rainforest?
questions.explanation: The Amazon Rainforest is located primarily in Brazil, South America.

questions.text: What is the term for seasonal movement of animals from one region to another?
questions.explanation: Migration is the regular seasonal movement of animals between breeding and wintering grounds.

questions.text: What is the gestation period of an African elephant?
questions.explanation: African elephants have the longest gestation period of any land mammal, at approximately 22 months.

questions.text: What is the name of the process by which a caterpillar transforms into a butterfly?
questions.explanation: Metamorphosis is the biological process of transformation; the pupal stage is part of complete metamorphosis.

questions.text: Which ecosystem has the highest biodiversity per unit area?
questions.explanation: Coral reefs are among the most biodiverse ecosystems on Earth, housing 25% of all marine species.

questions.text: What do male peacocks use their elaborate tail feathers for?
questions.explanation: Male peacocks fan their iridescent tail feathers to attract females during courtship displays.

questions.text: Which venomous mammal uses its rear leg spurs to inject venom?
questions.explanation: The male platypus has venomous spurs on its hind legs used primarily during competition with rivals.

questions.text: What is the largest species of penguin?
questions.explanation: The Emperor Penguin is the tallest and heaviest penguin species, standing up to 3.7 feet tall.

questions.text: Which animal has the longest lifespan in the wild?
questions.explanation: The Greenland Shark can live over 400 years, making it the longest-lived vertebrate known.

questions.text: What is the term for an animal that eats both plants and meat?
questions.explanation: An omnivore consumes both plant and animal matter; examples include bears, pigs, and humans.

questions.text: How do fireflies produce light?
questions.explanation: Fireflies produce light through bioluminescence, a chemical reaction involving luciferin and oxygen.

questions.text: Which bird has the longest wingspan of any living bird?
questions.explanation: The Wandering Albatross has the longest wingspan of any living bird, up to 11.5 feet across.

questions.text: What is the name of the symbiotic relationship where one organism benefits and the other is neither harmed nor helped?
questions.explanation: Commensalism is a relationship where one organism benefits while the other is unaffected.

questions.text: Which is the only continent where wolves are not found naturally?
questions.explanation: Australia has no native wolves; its large carnivores include the dingo (introduced) and Tasmanian devil.

questions.text: What is the primary purpose of a porcupine's quills?
questions.explanation: A porcupine's quills are modified hairs that serve as a defense mechanism against predators.

questions.text: What is the name of the migratory route used by monarch butterflies?
options: They do not use named routes
questions.explanation: Monarch butterflies travel up to 3,000 miles but their routes are not designated flyways like birds.

questions.text: Which ocean zone receives no sunlight?
questions.explanation: The midnight zone (below 1,000 meters) receives no sunlight at all, making it completely dark.

questions.text: What is the critically endangered status of the Amur leopard?
questions.explanation: The Amur leopard is Critically Endangered, with fewer than 100 adults remaining in the wild.

questions.text: Which insect is responsible for pollinating most of the world's crops?
questions.explanation: Honeybees are the world's most important pollinators, contributing to about one-third of the food supply.

questions.text: What feature makes a bird a bird (shared by all birds)?
questions.explanation: Feathers are the defining characteristic of birds; all birds have feathers, even flightless ones.

questions.text: What is the term for the relationship where both organisms benefit from each other?
questions.explanation: Mutualism is a symbiotic relationship in which both organisms benefit, such as bees and flowers.

questions.text: Approximately how many neurons does an octopus have in total?
questions.explanation: An octopus has about 500 million neurons, roughly two-thirds of which are in its arms.

questions.text: What is the scientific term for animals that maintain a constant body temperature regardless of their environment?
questions.explanation: Endothermic animals (like mammals and birds) generate their own body heat to maintain a constant temperature.

questions.text: Which species of whale is known for producing the most complex songs?
questions.explanation: Humpback whales are renowned for their long, complex songs, which can last for hours.

questions.text: What is the IUCN Red List status of the Javan Rhinoceros?
questions.explanation: The Javan Rhinoceros is Critically Endangered, with only about 70 individuals remaining.

questions.text: What is the process by which certain bacteria convert atmospheric nitrogen into a usable form?
questions.explanation: Nitrogen fixation is the conversion of atmospheric N₂ into ammonia by bacteria like Rhizobium.

questions.text: Which of these animals uses echolocation?
questions.explanation: Bats use echolocation — emitting high-frequency sounds and interpreting the echoes — to navigate and hunt.

questions.text: What is the approximate number of species of beetles, making them the largest insect order?
questions.explanation: Coleoptera (beetles) contains about 400,000 described species, more than any other order of animals.

questions.text: What is the name of the chemical process by which plants release water through their leaves?
questions.explanation: Transpiration is the process by which plants release water vapor through stomata in their leaves.

questions.text: Which ecosystem stores the most carbon per unit area?
questions.explanation: Peatlands store approximately twice as much carbon per unit area as any other terrestrial ecosystem.

questions.text: What is the primary reason the dodo became extinct?
questions.explanation: The dodo became extinct in the 17th century due to overhunting by sailors and predation by introduced animals.

questions.text: What is 'keystone species'?
options: The most numerous species in an ecosystem
A species whose impact on its ecosystem is disproportionately large
A species at the top of the food chain
A species endemic to a single region
questions.explanation: A keystone species has a disproportionately large effect on its ecosystem relative to its abundance.

questions.text: What is the scientific name for the domestic dog?
questions.explanation: The domestic dog's scientific name is Canis lupus familiaris, a subspecies of the gray wolf.

questions.text: What is the term for an animal species that exists only in one specific geographic area?
questions.explanation: An endemic species is native to and restricted to a particular geographic region.
